1: A shadow: 'I made a covenant with my eyes; how then could I look upon a young woman?' — sexual purity as covenantal integrity. Christ radicalized this: 'everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart' (Matthew 5:28). Job's covenant-with-eyes is the OT form of the heart-purity Christ requires and himself perfectly embodies.
2: A revelation of God: the 'portion God assigns from above' — the divine allocation of fate and reward. The NT grounds this in Christ: 'in him we have obtained an inheritance' (Ephesians 1:11), determined by divine purpose. The portion that comes 'from above' is secured through the one who himself came 'from above' (John 3:31).
3: A revelation of God: 'is there not ruin for the unrighteous?' — the retribution principle that underlies Job's oath. The NT confirms the principle while transforming its application: the ruin deserved by the unrighteous fell on Christ, 'the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring us to God' (1 Peter 3:18). The principle stands; the one who bore it is unexpected.
4: A revelation of God: 'does he not see my ways and number all my steps?' — the divine omniscience as the ground of Job's oath. The NT confirms and personalizes this: 'even the hairs of your head are all numbered' (Matthew 10:30). The same all-seeing God before whom Job stands is the Father who knows every detail of our lives.
5: A theme: 'if I have walked in falsehood, or my foot has rushed toward deceit' — the moral audit of one's path. Christ is 'the way' (John 14:6) — not just the destination but the pattern of walking. The one who invites disciples to walk in his way is himself the one who never rushed toward deceit.
6: A theme: 'let me be weighed in an honest balance, so that God may know my integrity' — the appeal to divine assessment. The NT's most searching passage on divine assessment is 2 Corinthians 5:10: 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.' But those in Christ appear wearing his righteousness, not their own — the balance is honest, and the one who stands in their place has already been weighed.
7: A theme: 'if my heart has followed my eyes, or any stain has clung to my hand' — the moral contamination that spreads from sight to heart to deed. The NT's diagnosis is the same: 'out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery...' (Matthew 15:19). The sequence Job fears — sight corrupting heart corrupting action — is the sequence the gospel addresses at its root, by transforming the heart.
8: A theme: 'let me plant and another harvest' — the imprecation of displacement. The NT's positive form: 'one sows and another reaps' (John 4:37) describes the missionary pattern where labor benefits those who did not labor. The displacement Job invokes as curse becomes, in the gospel's economy, a description of the generous overflow of blessing.
9: A theme: the prohibition against adultery — 'if my heart has been drawn away by a woman.' Job's oath of marital fidelity is the OT foundation of the NT's teaching on covenant marriage (Matthew 19:4-6, Ephesians 5:25-33). Christ's faithfulness to his bride, the church, is the theological grounding of the marital covenant Job here affirms.
10: A shadow: 'let my wife grind for another man' — the self-imprecation of maximum social shame. The NT transforms the marriage metaphor: Christ's relationship to the church is that of the faithful husband (Ephesians 5:25-27), who 'loved the church and gave himself up for her.' The shame Job invokes for infidelity is the mirror-image of the faithfulness Christ embodies.
11: A revelation of God: 'for that would be a shameful outrage and an iniquity deserving judgment' — the naming of sexual sin as a breach of justice, not merely decorum. The NT confirms this: 'flee from sexual immorality' is the command, grounded in the theology of the body as the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:18-20). The body belongs to God, which is why its violation is an iniquity.
12: A shadow: 'a fire that burns all the way to Abaddon' — the consequence of lust reaching into the realm of destruction. The NT's warning is parallel: 'if your right eye causes you to sin, tear it out and throw it away. For it is better that you lose one of your members than that your whole body be thrown into hell' (Matthew 5:29). The fire that reaches Abaddon is the same fire Christ warns against.
13: A revelation of God: 'if I have dismissed the complaint of my servant or maidservant when they had a dispute with me' — the obligation of justice to those under one's authority. The NT grounds this in the incarnation: God himself took the form of a servant (Philippians 2:7), making every servant's complaint a matter of divine concern. The master who dismisses a servant's case dismisses it before the one who became a servant.
14: A revelation of God: 'what could I do when God rises up? when he holds inquiry, what shall I answer him?' — the anticipation of divine scrutiny. The NT's comfort: those in Christ 'will not come into judgment' (John 5:24) — the inquiry that Job fears as exposing potential failure has already been conducted, and the verdict rendered: no condemnation (Romans 8:1).
15: A revelation of God: 'did not the one who made me in the womb make him too? did not one God form us both?' — the common humanity of master and servant, grounded in common creation. The NT's fullest expression: 'there is neither slave nor free... for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28). The equality Job asserts creatorially, Paul grounds in the new creation inaugurated by Christ.
16: A revelation of God: 'if I have refused the poor what they needed, or caused the widow's eyes to fail' — the obligation to the poor and widow. The NT names care for the poor and orphan as 'religion that is pure and undefiled' (James 1:27). Christ's own ministry was characterized by concern for the poor (Luke 4:18); his body continues it.
17: A revelation of God: 'if I ate my food alone and the fatherless ate nothing' — the obligation to share one's table. The NT's eucharistic community is defined by this sharing: the early church had 'all things in common... distributing to each as any had need' (Acts 2:44-45). The closed table Job swears against is the antithesis of Christ's open one.
18: A revelation of God: 'from my youth the fatherless grew up with me as with a father' — a lifetime of care for the vulnerable. The NT names God as 'Father of the fatherless' (Psalms 68:5) and grounds adoption into his family in Christ's work (Romans 8:15). Job's fatherly care for the fatherless mirrors the divine fatherhood that the gospel makes accessible.
19: A theme: 'if I have seen anyone dying from lack of clothing' — the obligation to clothe the naked. The NT's judgment scene turns on exactly this: 'I was naked and you clothed me... as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me' (Matthew 25:36,40). The naked stranger is Christ; Job's oath is the life Christ commends at the judgment.
20: A revelation of God: 'if the wool of my sheep has not kept him warm' — care that comes from one's own resources. The NT's good shepherd 'lays down his life for the sheep' (John 10:11) — giving not just wool but himself. Job's generous self-giving from his own resources is the creaturely form of the divine self-giving in Christ.
21: A shadow: 'if I have raised my hand against the fatherless when I had allies at the city gate' — the abuse of power against the defenseless. The NT's condemnation of power used against the vulnerable is sharp (Luke 20:47: scribes who 'devour widows' houses'). Christ, who had all power, used it entirely on behalf of the defenseless.
22: A shadow: 'let my arm fall from its socket and be broken from the elbow' — the self-imprecation against arm-violence. The NT's parallel: Christ stretched out his arms on the cross — not to strike but to bear. The arms that Job swears will be broken if used unjustly were, in Christ, stretched out in the ultimate act of non-retaliatory self-giving.
23: A revelation of God: 'for the destruction God sends is a terror to me; before his majesty I cannot hold my ground' — the divine majesty as the ground of Job's ethical restraint. The NT's basis for ethics is the same: 'since we know the fear of the Lord, we persuade others' (2 Corinthians 5:11). The awe that motivates Job's integrity is taken up into the NT's theology of the divine majesty revealed in Christ.
24: A theme: 'if I have made gold my hope or called pure gold my security' — the prohibition of wealth as ultimate trust. The NT's parallel: 'you cannot serve God and money' (Matthew 6:24); 'it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God' (Matthew 19:24). Job's oath against making gold his security is the OT form of Christ's teaching on mammon.
25: A theme: 'if I rejoiced that my wealth was great or that my hand had gained much' — the spiritual danger of satisfaction in abundance. The NT's beatitude is the reversal: 'blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven' (Matthew 5:3). The one who does not rejoice in his own hand's gain can receive the kingdom as gift.
26: A theme: 'if I gazed at the sun in its brilliance or the moon as it moved in splendor' — the prohibition of star and sun worship. The NT places Christ above all cosmic objects: he is 'the bright morning star' (Revelation 22:16), and in the new creation 'the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb' (Revelation 21:23). The objects of potential idolatry are subordinate to the one the gospel reveals.
27: A theme: 'my heart was secretly seduced, and my hand was kissed toward them' — the inner movement toward idolatry, the heart drawn away before the hand acts. The NT locates idolatry in the heart: 'covetousness... is idolatry' (Colossians 3:5). The secret seduction of the heart toward anything other than God is the root of idolatry that the gospel addresses by redirecting the heart's affection toward Christ.
28: A revelation of God: 'for I would have denied the God above' — the equation of cosmic idolatry with the denial of the true God. The NT's confession is the positive form: 'Jesus is Lord' (Romans 10:9) — the confession that the world's objects of idolatry (wealth, power, cosmic forces) do not hold final allegiance. To confess Christ is to deny the idols.
29: A revelation of God: 'if I rejoiced when my enemy was ruined or exulted when disaster found him' — the prohibition of schadenfreude. The NT's command is the positive version: 'love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you' (Matthew 5:44). Christ prays from the cross for those who are executing him (Luke 23:34). Job's oath against enemy-joy is the minimum; Christ's active love for enemies is the fulfillment.
30: A revelation of God: 'I have not let my mouth sin by calling down a curse on his life' — the restrained tongue that does not curse enemies. The NT's positive: 'bless those who curse you, pray for those who abuse you' (Luke 6:28). Christ himself is the example: 'when he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten' (1 Peter 2:23). Job's non-cursing is the threshold; Christ's active blessing is the fulfillment.
31: A revelation of God: 'who has not been filled with his food?' — the testimony of household generosity, confirmed by Job's own servants. The feeding motif is central to Christ's ministry: the miraculous feedings (Mark 6:42: 'they all ate and were satisfied'), the parables of banquets, the eucharist. The question 'who has not been filled?' is answered by those who ate with Jesus.
32: A revelation of God: 'no stranger had to sleep in the street; I opened my doors to the traveler' — radical hospitality. The NT's extension: 'do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares' (Hebrews 13:2). Christ himself was 'a stranger' — 'he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11) — but those who receive him receive the Father (Matthew 10:40).
33: A type: 'if I have hidden my transgressions like Adam, concealing my iniquity in my heart' — the Adamic pattern of concealment after sin. Genesis 3:8-10 is the prototype: Adam hides from God after the fall. Christ is the second Adam (Romans 5:14-15) who does not hide but fully discloses himself: 'I am the way and the truth and the life' (John 14:6). Concealment is the Adamic response; transparent self-giving is the Christic reversal.
34: A revelation of God: 'because I feared the crowd and the contempt of families terrified me, so I kept silent' — the capitulation to social pressure that Job swears he has avoided. The NT's opposite is the martyr-witness: 'do not fear those who kill the body' (Matthew 10:28). Christ before Pilate does not keep silent to preserve himself; he speaks truth to power (John 18:36-37). The fear Job overcame in his integrity, Christ overcame in his Passion.
35: A type: 'O that someone would hear me! here is my signature — let the Almighty answer me!' — the legal demand for a divine response. The divine speeches in chapters 38-41 answer this demand, though not in the way Job expected. In the NT, the ultimate divine answer to humanity's legal demand is the cross: God speaks in the death and resurrection of his Son. The signature Job produces is answered by the one who bears scars in hands and side (John 20:27).
36: A shadow: 'I would carry it on my shoulder and wear it as a crown on my head' — the paradox of bearing one's indictment as a mark of honor. The NT's parallel: the crown of thorns (John 19:2) placed mockingly on Christ's head becomes, in the resurrection's light, the sign of his true kingship. What is given in contempt is transformed into honor.
37: A shadow: 'I would approach him like a prince' — the confidence of approaching God as one entitled to his audience. The NT's language for the believer's access to God: 'with confidence draw near to the throne of grace' (Hebrews 4:16), not as a suppliant begging for attention but as those who have a right of access through Christ. Job's princely confidence is available to those who come through the one who is himself the Prince of Peace (Isaiah 9:6).
38: A theme: 'if my land has cried out against me and its furrows have wept' — the land bearing witness to its owner's conduct. The NT holds that creation itself testifies (Romans 1:20) and groans (Romans 8:22); the land's witness is real. The creation that cries out against those who abuse it waits for 'the revealing of the sons of God' (Romans 8:19) — the restoration Christ will bring.
39: A revelation of God: 'if I have eaten its produce without paying, or stripped the life from its owners' — the economic obligation of just dealing. The NT's equivalent: Zacchaeus, transformed by Jesus, restores fourfold what he has taken (Luke 19:8). The justice of honest economic dealing is what the encounter with Christ produces, not merely commands.
40: A theme: 'then let thistles grow instead of wheat, and weeds instead of barley' — the curse of the ground as the consequence of injustice. Genesis 3:17-18 is the background: thorns and thistles are the curse-sign of the fall. The NT holds that Christ 'redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13) — he wore the thorns as his crown. The words of Job are ended at this verse, in a fitting image of what Christ absorbed.
